# Level 13 — Advanced R Programming {.unnumbered}

::: {.pbl-box .pbl-problem}
**Where this level is headed**

Level 9 taught you to write functions; Level 10 taught you to automate repeated tasks with loops. This level goes one layer deeper: *how R actually keeps track of variables behind the scenes*, how to build functions that remember state between calls, how to handle errors gracefully instead of letting a script crash, and how to work with the two data types nearly every real dataset eventually needs — text patterns and dates — that this course's mostly-numeric dataset hasn't required yet.
:::

## What this level covers

- Environments — how R actually stores and looks up variables, and why a function's variables normally disappear once it finishes
- Closures — functions that "remember" values from where they were created, enabling reusable function factories
- A first look at R's S3 object system, for building your own structured result types
- Robust error handling with `tryCatch()`, beyond Level 9's `stop()` and `warning()`
- Debugging tools for finding *why* code fails, not just that it failed
- Functional programming patterns like `Reduce()` for combining values one at a time
- Regular expressions for detecting and extracting text patterns
- General string manipulation beyond what Levels 1-12 have needed
- Working with dates — parsing, formatting, and calculating differences
- The basic anatomy of an R package, for turning reusable functions into shareable tools

::: {.pbl-box .pbl-mission}
**A preview mission**

Your mission across this level: build a small, reusable toolkit — a function factory for flexible threshold checks, a custom result type, and an error-safe file reader — and bring it all together in a capstone that automates reading and validating a folder of clinic files, extending Level 10's automation with real error handling.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## A note on scope

This level focuses on the R-programming skills that generalize far beyond this course's dataset: environments, closures, error handling, debugging, functional patterns, regex, strings, dates, and package structure. Deeper object-oriented systems (S4, R6) and full package publishing workflows (roxygen2 documentation generation, CRAN submission, Git/GitHub collaboration) are mentioned where relevant but are beyond this course's scope — Level 10's `browser()`-free debugging and this level's S3 introduction give you a solid, practical foundation to build on later.
:::
